title: Condensation graph: build DAG of SCCs.
description: Condensation graph: build DAG of SCCs.

examples.explanation: Step 1: Find reachable sets via DFS from each vertex. From 0: {0,1,2,3} (0ŌåÆ1ŌåÆ2ŌåÆ0ŌåÆ... and 1ŌåÆ3). From 1: {1,2,0,3}. From 2: {2,0,1,3}. From 3: {3}. Step 2: Check mutual reachability. 0 reaches 1 and 1 reaches 0 ŌåÆ same SCC. Similarly 0-2 and 1-2 are mutually reachable. 3 only reaches itself. So SCCs: {0,1,2} and {3}. Step 3: Build condensation graph. Original edges: 0ŌåÆ1 (both in SCC1, ignore), 1ŌåÆ2 (both in SCC1, ignore), 2ŌåÆ0 (both in SCC1, ignore), 1ŌåÆ3 (SCC1ŌåÆSCC2) ŌåÆ add edge SCC1ŌåÆSCC2. Result: 2 SCC nodes with one edge.

Step 1: Find reachable sets. From 0: {0,1,2}. From 1: {1,2}. From 2: {2}. Step 2: Check mutual reachability. 0 reaches 1 but 1 doesn't reach 0 ŌåÆ different SCCs. 1 reaches 2 but 2 doesn't reach 1 ŌåÆ different SCCs. So each vertex is its own SCC: {0}, {1}, {2}. Step 3: Build condensation graph. Original edges: 0ŌåÆ1 (SCC0ŌåÆSCC1) add edge, 1ŌåÆ2 (SCC1ŌåÆSCC2) add edge. Result: 3 SCC nodes with edges 0ŌåÆ1 and 1ŌåÆ2.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(V*(V+E))
approaches.summary: For each vertex, perform DFS to find all reachable vertices, then group vertices that are mutually reachable into SCCs. Build the condensation graph by creating nodes for each SCC and adding edges between SCCs if there is an edge from any vertex in one SCC to any vertex in another. This is straightforward but inefficient for large graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem ŌĆö Build Condensation Graph of SCCs using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of condensation graph construction using O(V*(V+E)) brute force SCC detection
ladders.explanation: The condensation graph represents a directed graph where each strongly connected component (SCC) becomes a single node, and edges between SCCs represent connections between components. The brute force approach works by first identifying all SCCs through mutual reachability checks, then constructing the condensed DAG. For each vertex, we perform DFS to find all reachable vertices (O(V+E) per vertex). Two vertices are in the same SCC if each can reach the other (mutual reachability). We group vertices into SCCs by checking these mutual reachability relationships. Once SCCs are identified, we build the condensation graph: create one node per SCC, then for each edge (u,v) in the original graph, if u and v belong to different SCCs, add an edge between those SCCs in the condensation graph (avoiding duplicates). The intuition is straightforward: SCCs are maximal sets of mutually reachable vertices, and the condensation graph captures the reachability structure between these components. This approach is conceptually simple but inefficient for large graphs due to the O(V*(V+E)) time complexity from repeated DFS traversals. Space complexity is O(V+E) for storing the graph and reachability matrices. Common mistakes include not handling self-loops properly in SCC detection, forgetting to check mutual reachability (not just one-way reachability), and creating duplicate edges in the condensation graph. The connection to real applications is in understanding graph connectivity in systems like web page links or dependency graphs.

examples.trace: Step 1: Find reachable sets via DFS from each vertex. From 0: {0,1,2,3} (0ŌåÆ1ŌåÆ2ŌåÆ0ŌåÆ... and 1ŌåÆ3). From 1: {1,2,0,3}. From 2: {2,0,1,3}. From 3: {3}. Step 2: Check mutual reachability. 0 reaches 1 and 1 reaches 0 ŌåÆ same SCC. Similarly 0-2 and 1-2 are mutually reachable. 3 only reaches itself. So SCCs: {0,1,2} and {3}. Step 3: Build condensation graph. Original edges: 0ŌåÆ1 (both in SCC1, ignore), 1ŌåÆ2 (both in SCC1, ignore), 2ŌåÆ0 (both in SCC1, ignore), 1ŌåÆ3 (SCC1ŌåÆSCC2) ŌåÆ add edge SCC1ŌåÆSCC2. Result: 2 SCC nodes with one edge.

Step 1: Find reachable sets. From 0: {0,1,2}. From 1: {1,2}. From 2: {2}. Step 2: Check mutual reachability. 0 reaches 1 but 1 doesn't reach 0 ŌåÆ different SCCs. 1 reaches 2 but 2 doesn't reach 1 ŌåÆ different SCCs. So each vertex is its own SCC: {0}, {1}, {2}. Step 3: Build condensation graph. Original edges: 0ŌåÆ1 (SCC0ŌåÆSCC1) add edge, 1ŌåÆ2 (SCC1ŌåÆSCC2) add edge. Result: 3 SCC nodes with edges 0ŌåÆ1 and 1ŌåÆ2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine ŌĆö Find All SCCs via Mutual Reachability Checking
ladders.desc: Identify strongly connected components by checking pairwise mutual reachability between vertices
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the core of the brute force approach: finding all SCCs in a directed graph. An SCC is defined as a maximal set of vertices where every pair of vertices is mutually reachable (each can reach the other via directed paths). The algorithm works by first computing reachability from each vertex using DFS or BFS, storing which vertices are reachable from each starting point. Then, we iterate through all pairs of vertices (or use a more efficient grouping method) to determine which vertices belong to the same SCC. Specifically, two vertices u and v are in the same SCC if u can reach v AND v can reach u. We can build SCCs by starting with any vertex, finding all vertices that are mutually reachable with it, marking them as one SCC, and repeating for unvisited vertices. The intuition is that reachability is transitive within an SCC: if a reaches b and b reaches c, and all are mutually reachable, they form one SCC. Time complexity is O(V*(V+E)) because we perform V DFS/BFS traversals (each O(V+E)) to compute reachability, then O(V┬▓) for pairwise checks (though this can be optimized during grouping). Space complexity is O(V┬▓) if storing full reachability matrix, or O(V+E) if recomputing reachability during checks. Common mistakes include confusing one-way reachability with mutual reachability, not handling self-reachability correctly (a vertex always reaches itself), and inefficiently recomputing reachability multiple times. This sub-routine feeds directly into the full problem by providing the SCC groupings needed to build the condensation graph.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute reachability via DFS from each vertex. From 0: DFS visits 0ŌåÆ1ŌåÆ2ŌåÆ0 (cycle) ŌåÆ reachable set {0,1,2}. From 1: 1ŌåÆ2ŌåÆ0ŌåÆ1 ŌåÆ {0,1,2}. From 2: 2ŌåÆ0ŌåÆ1ŌåÆ2 ŌåÆ {0,1,2}. Step 2: Check mutual reachability. For vertices 0,1,2: 0 reaches 1 and 1 reaches 0 ŌåÆ mutually reachable. Similarly all pairs are mutually reachable. Step 3: Group into SCCs. All three vertices are mutually reachable ŌåÆ one SCC {0,1,2}.

Step 1: Compute reachability. From 0: {0,1,2}. From 1: {1,2}. From 2: {1,2}. From 3: {3,0,1,2}. Step 2: Check mutual reachability. 0 reaches 1 but 1 doesn't reach 0 ŌåÆ different SCCs. 1 reaches 2 and 2 reaches 1 ŌåÆ same SCC. 3 reaches 0 but 0 doesn't reach 3 ŌåÆ different. Step 3: Group: {0} alone, {1,2} together, {3} alone.

ladders.title: Core Logic ŌĆö Compute Reachability Sets via DFS from Each Vertex
ladders.desc: Perform DFS from every vertex to determine all vertices reachable from each starting point
ladders.explanation: This core logic computes, for each vertex in the graph, the set of all vertices that can be reached from it via directed paths. This information is essential for determining mutual reachability in SCC detection. The algorithm is straightforward: for each vertex v in the graph, perform a Depth-First Search (DFS) starting from v, marking all visited vertices as reachable from v. We maintain a visited array for each DFS to avoid cycles causing infinite recursion, and we record the reachable vertices in a set or boolean matrix. The intuition is that reachability captures the transitive closure of the graph's edges: if there's a path from u to v (possibly through intermediate vertices), then v is reachable from u. In directed graphs, reachability is not symmetric (u reaching v doesn't imply v reaches u), which is why we need to check both directions for SCCs. Time complexity is O(V*(V+E)) because we run V separate DFS traversals, each potentially visiting all V vertices and E edges in worst case (though actual complexity depends on graph density). Space complexity is O(V+E) for the graph representation plus O(V) for the visited array per DFS, or O(V┬▓) if storing all reachability sets explicitly. Common mistakes include not resetting the visited array between DFS calls, handling cycles incorrectly (DFS with proper visited marking handles cycles fine), and confusion about directed vs undirected reachability. This logic feeds into the SCC detection by providing the raw reachability data needed to check mutual reachability between vertices.

examples.trace: Step 1: DFS from vertex 0. Start at 0, mark visited. Neighbors: 1. Go to 1, mark visited. Neighbors: 2. Go to 2, mark visited. Neighbors: none. Backtrack. Reachable set from 0: {0,1,2}. Step 2: Reset visited, DFS from 1. Start at 1, mark visited. Neighbors: 2. Go to 2, mark visited. Neighbors: none. Reachable set from 1: {1,2}. Step 3: Reset visited, DFS from 2. Start at 2, mark visited. Neighbors: none. Reachable set from 2: {2}.

Step 1: DFS from 0. Start 0ŌåÆ1ŌåÆ0 (cycle, but visited prevents re-entry)ŌåÆ2. Reachable: {0,1,2}. Step 2: DFS from 1. Start 1ŌåÆ0ŌåÆ1 (cycle)ŌåÆ2. Reachable: {0,1,2}. Step 3: DFS from 2. Start 2, no neighbors. Reachable: {2}.

ladders.title: Building Block ŌĆö Depth-First Search (DFS) on Directed Graphs
ladders.desc: Traverse a directed graph using DFS to visit all reachable vertices from a starting point
ladders.explanation: Depth-First Search is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. In directed graphs, DFS follows the direction of edges. The algorithm starts at a given source vertex, marks it as visited, then recursively visits all unvisited neighbors. For each neighbor, the process repeats. This continues until all vertices reachable from the source have been visited. We maintain a visited set (usually a boolean array) to avoid revisiting vertices and prevent infinite loops in cyclic graphs. The intuition is that DFS mimics exploring a maze by always taking the first available path, going deeper until hitting a dead end, then backtracking to try other paths. In directed graphs, edges have direction, so we only follow edges from the current vertex to its outgoing neighbors. Time complexity for DFS from one source is O(V+E) in adjacency list representation, as each vertex and edge is processed at most once. Space complexity is O(V) for the visited array and recursion stack (which can be O(V) in worst-case linear graphs). Common mistakes include not marking vertices as visited before recursing (leading to infinite recursion in cycles), confusing directed with undirected traversal, and not handling disconnected graphs properly. This building block is essential for computing reachability sets, as each DFS from a vertex identifies all vertices reachable from that starting point.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at vertex 0, mark visited. Neighbors: 1 and 2. Choose first neighbor 1. Step 2: Go to vertex 1, mark visited. Neighbors: 3. Step 3: Go to vertex 3, mark visited. Neighbors: none. Backtrack to 1. Step 4: Backtrack to 0. Next unvisited neighbor: 2. Step 5: Go to vertex 2, mark visited. Neighbors: none. Done. Visited order: 0,1,3,2.

Step 1: Start at 0, mark visited. Neighbors: 1. Step 2: Go to 1, mark visited. Neighbors: 2. Step 3: Go to 2, mark visited. Neighbors: 0, but 0 already visited, so skip. Backtrack to 1, then to 0. Done. Order: 0,1,2. Note: The cycle 2ŌåÆ0 doesn't cause re-traversal because 0 is already visited.

testCases.expected: Traversal: [0,1,2] or [0,2,1] (order depends on neighbor processing)

ladders.title: Basic Operation ŌĆö Graph Representation and Neighbor Iteration
ladders.desc: Represent a directed graph and iterate through neighbors of a vertex
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two key parts: representing a directed graph in memory, and accessing/iterating through the neighbors of a given vertex. The most common representation for graph algorithms is adjacency list: an array of lists, where each index corresponds to a vertex, and the list at that index contains all vertices that are direct neighbors via outgoing edges. For example, if there's an edge from vertex u to vertex v, then v appears in the adjacency list of u. This representation is space-efficient for sparse graphs (O(V+E)) and allows quick access to neighbors. The operation of iterating through neighbors simply means looping through the list associated with a vertex. The intuition is that graphs model relationships (like web links or social connections), and adjacency lists capture these relationships in a way that's easy to traverse. Time complexity for iterating neighbors of one vertex is O(degree(v)), where degree is the number of outgoing edges. Space complexity for the graph representation is O(V+E). Common mistakes include using adjacency matrix for sparse graphs (wasting space), confusing incoming vs outgoing edges, and not handling duplicate edges properly. This operation is foundational for DFS and other graph traversals, as DFS needs to access neighbors of each visited vertex to continue the traversal.

examples.input: Graph: 3 vertices, edges: 0ŌåÆ1, 0ŌåÆ2, iterate neighbors of vertex 0
examples.trace: Step 1: Represent graph as adjacency list: vertex 0: [1,2], vertex 1: [], vertex 2: []. Step 2: To iterate neighbors of vertex 0, access the list at index 0: [1,2]. Step 3: Process neighbors in order: first neighbor 1, then neighbor 2.

examples.input: Graph: 4 vertices, edges: 0ŌåÆ1, 1ŌåÆ2, 2ŌåÆ3, 3ŌåÆ1, iterate neighbors of vertex 1
examples.trace: Step 1: Adjacency list: 0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[3], 3:[1]. Step 2: For vertex 1, access list at index 1: [2]. Step 3: Iterate: only neighbor is 2.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation ŌĆö Directed Graphs and Vertices/Edges
ladders.desc: Understand basic directed graph concepts: vertices, directed edges, and simple graph examples
ladders.explanation: A directed graph (digraph) is a collection of vertices (also called nodes) and directed edges (also called arcs). Each vertex represents an entity (like a person, web page, or task), and each directed edge represents a one-way relationship from one vertex to another (like a link from one web page to another, or a dependency where one task must precede another). Directed edges are ordered pairs: an edge from vertex u to vertex v is written as (u,v) or uŌåÆv, indicating direction from u to v. This is different from undirected graphs where edges have no direction. Key properties: vertices are typically labeled with integers (0,1,2,...) for simplicity; edges can be listed as pairs; a vertex can have zero or more outgoing edges (edges starting from it) and zero or more incoming edges (edges ending at it); self-loops (edges from a vertex to itself) are possible but often not considered in basic SCC problems. The intuition is that directed graphs model asymmetric relationships where the direction matters (like following someone on social media doesn't mean they follow you back). This foundation is essential because SCCs and condensation graphs are defined on directed graphsŌĆöthe direction of edges determines reachability. Common mistakes include confusing directed with undirected edges, misinterpreting edge direction, and not understanding that vertices are distinct entities. This concept underpins all higher-level operations, as graph algorithms require correct understanding of the graph structure.

examples.output: Graph visualization: 0 points to 1, 1 points to 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify vertices: three vertices labeled 0, 1, and 2. Step 2: Identify edges: first edge from vertex 0 to vertex 1. This means if you start at 0, you can go to 1, but not vice-versa (unless another edge exists). Second edge from vertex 1 to vertex 2: from 1 to 2. Step 3: Understand reachability: from 0, you can reach 1 and 2 (via 1). From 1, you can reach 2. From 2, you cannot reach 0 or 1 (no outgoing edges to them).

examples.output: Graph visualization: 0 and 1 point to each other
examples.trace: Step 1: Vertices: 0 and 1. Step 2: Edges: 0ŌåÆ1 means from 0 to 1; 1ŌåÆ0 means from 1 to 0. Step 3: This creates a two-way relationship: from 0 you can reach 1, and from 1 you can reach 0. This mutual reachability is key for SCCs.

testCases.expected: Empty graph: no vertices or edges
Single vertex graph: vertex 0 with no edges
Two vertices: edge from 0 to 1 only
Three vertices: 0 points to both 1 and 2
Three vertices: 0 and 1 bidirectional, 1 points to 2
Four vertices: 1 and 2 have a mutual edge, 3 points to 0

approaches.name: Kosaraju's Algorithm O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Perform two DFS passes: first on the original graph to compute finishing times, then on the transpose graph to identify SCCs based on those times. Build the condensation graph by mapping each SCC to a node and adding edges between SCCs based on original graph edges. This approach is efficient and widely used for directed graphs.

ladders.title: Full Problem ŌĆö Build Condensation Graph Using Kosaraju's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find SCCs and construct the DAG of condensed components.
ladders.explanation: The full problem requires implementing Kosaraju's algorithm to find strongly connected components (SCCs) in a directed graph and then building the condensation graph where each SCC becomes a single node. The intuition is that by performing two DFS passesŌĆöfirst on the original graph to compute finishing times, then on the transpose graph to identify SCCs based on those timesŌĆöwe can efficiently partition the graph. The condensation graph is constructed by mapping each SCC to a node and adding edges between SCCs based on original graph edges, ensuring no edges within the same SCC to maintain DAG structure. Step-by-step: 1) Create adjacency lists for the graph and its transpose. 2) Perform first DFS on the original graph to fill a stack with vertices in order of finishing times. 3) Perform second DFS on the transpose graph, popping vertices from the stack to identify SCCs. 4) Assign each vertex an SCC ID. 5) Build the condensation graph by iterating through original edges and adding edges between different SCCs. Time complexity is O(V+E) for both DFS passes and edge processing. Space complexity is O(V+E) for adjacency lists and auxiliary data structures. This feeds into graph analysis by simplifying complex directed graphs into DAGs for easier traversal. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs properly or adding self-loops in the condensation graph. Real code pattern: Use arrays for visited flags, stack for order, and dictionaries for SCC mapping.

examples.output: Condensation graph: SCCs: {0,1,2} as node A, {3} as node B; edges: A->B
examples.trace: Step 1: Original graph adjacency: 0:[1], 1:[2,3], 2:[0], 3:[]. Transpose: 0:[2], 1:[0], 2:[1], 3:[1]. Step 2: First DFS on original: start at 0, visit 1, then 2 (back to 0, cycle), then 3. Stack order: 3, 0, 2, 1. Step 3: Second DFS on transpose: pop 1, DFS from 1 visits 0 and 2 ŌåÆ SCC1={0,1,2}. Pop 0 and 2 already visited. Pop 3, DFS from 3 visits no others ŌåÆ SCC2={3}. Step 4: SCC IDs: 0:A, 1:A, 2:A, 3:B. Step 5: Build condensation: edges 0->1 (both A, skip), 1->2 (both A, skip), 2->0 (both A, skip), 1->3 (A->B, add edge). Result: nodes A and B with edge A->B.

examples.output: Condensation graph: SCCs: {0} as A, {1} as B, {2} as C; edges: A->B, B->C
examples.trace: Step 1: Original: 0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[]. Transpose: 0:[], 1:[0], 2:[1]. Step 2: First DFS: start at 0, visit 1, then 2. Stack: 2, 1, 0. Step 3: Second DFS: pop 0, DFS from 0 in transpose visits none ŌåÆ SCC1={0}. Pop 1, DFS from 1 visits 0 (already visited) ŌåÆ SCC2={1}. Pop 2, DFS from 2 visits 1 (already visited) ŌåÆ SCC3={2}. Step 4: IDs: 0:A, 1:B, 2:C. Step 5: Edges: 0->1 (A->B, add), 1->2 (B->C, add). Result: nodes A,B,C with edges A->B and B->C.

testCases.expected: Condensation graph: no nodes, no edges
Condensation graph: node A from {0}, no edges
Condensation graph: nodes A={0}, B={1}, edge A->B
Condensation graph: node A={0,1}, no edges
Condensation graph: node A={0,1,2}, no edges
Condensation graph: node A={0,1,2,3}, no edges
Condensation graph: nodes A={0}, B={1,2}, C={3}, edges: A->B, B->C
Condensation graph: nodes A={0,1,2}, B={3,4}, edge A->B
Condensation graph: nodes A={0,1,2}, B={3,4,5}, no edges
Condensation graph: nodes A={0}, B={1}, C={2}, D={3}, edges: A->B, A->C, B->D, C->D

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine ŌĆö Identify SCCs Using Two DFS Passes
ladders.desc: Implement the core SCC detection phase of Kosaraju's algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on finding all strongly connected components (SCCs) in a directed graph using Kosaraju's two-pass DFS method. The intuition is that by first computing finishing times in the original graph and then exploring the transpose graph in reverse order of those times, we can isolate SCCs efficiently. This works because the transpose graph reverses edge directions, and processing in reverse finishing time order ensures we start DFS from sink components in the original graph, correctly grouping reachable vertices into SCCs. Step-by-step: 1) Build adjacency lists for the graph and its transpose. 2) Perform DFS on the original graph to fill a stack with vertices in post-order (after visiting all neighbors). 3) Reset visited flags. 4) Perform DFS on the transpose graph, popping vertices from the stack; each DFS call from an unvisited vertex identifies one SCC. Time complexity is O(V+E) for the two DFS traversals. Space complexity is O(V+E) for adjacency lists and O(V) for stack and visited arrays. This feeds into the full problem by providing the SCC partitioning needed to build the condensation graph. Common mistakes include incorrect stack ordering or not properly resetting visited flags between passes. Real code pattern: Use recursion or iteration for DFS, with a stack to store order.

examples.trace: Step 1: Original: 0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[0]. Transpose: 0:[2], 1:[0], 2:[1]. Step 2: First DFS on original: start at 0, visit 1, then 2 (back to 0). Stack order: after DFS, push when done: 2, 1, 0. Step 3: Reset visited. Step 4: Second DFS on transpose: pop 0, DFS from 0 visits 2 and 1 ŌåÆ all visited, SCC={0,1,2}. Pop 1 and 2 already visited. Result: one SCC.

Step 1: Original: 0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[]. Transpose: 0:[], 1:[0], 2:[1]. Step 2: First DFS: start at 0, visit 1, then 2. Stack: 2, 1, 0. Step 3: Reset visited. Step 4: Second DFS: pop 0, DFS from 0 in transpose visits none ŌåÆ SCC1={0}. Pop 1, DFS from 1 visits 0 (already visited) ŌåÆ SCC2={1}. Pop 2, DFS from 2 visits 1 (already visited) ŌåÆ SCC3={2}. Result: three SCCs.

ladders.title: Core Logic ŌĆö DFS with Finishing Time Order
ladders.desc: Perform DFS to compute vertices in order of finishing times for SCC detection.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves performing a depth-first search (DFS) on a directed graph to record vertices in the order of their finishing times (i.e., when all descendants have been visited). The intuition is that in Kosaraju's algorithm, this ordering ensures that when we later process the transpose graph, we start from vertices that are in sink components, allowing us to correctly identify SCCs. This works because in a directed graph, vertices in the same SCC will have interdependent reachability, and processing in reverse finishing time order isolates these groups. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize a visited array of size V. 2) For each unvisited vertex, perform DFS: mark as visited, recursively visit all neighbors, and after returning from all neighbors, push the vertex onto a stack. 3) The stack will contain vertices in increasing order of finishing times (top is last finished). Time complexity is O(V+E) for the DFS traversal. Space complexity is O(V) for the stack and visited array, plus O(E) for adjacency list if not pre-existing. This feeds into the SCC detection by providing the order for the second DFS pass. Common mistakes include pushing vertices before visiting neighbors or not handling disconnected graphs. Real code pattern: Use recursion with a post-order push to stack.

examples.trace: Step 1: Visited all false. Step 2: DFS from 0: mark visited, go to neighbor 1. DFS from 1: mark visited, go to neighbor 2. DFS from 2: mark visited, no neighbors, push 2 to stack, return to 1. After 2 done, push 1 to stack, return to 0. After 1 done, push 0 to stack. Stack: bottom [0,1,2] top, so order for processing: pop 2 first.

examples.output: Stack order: [0,1,2] or [1,0,2] depending on start
examples.trace: Step 1: Visited false. Step 2: Start at 0: DFS from 0 visits 1, which visits 0 (already visited), so push 1 then 0. Or start at 2: DFS from 2 visits itself (cycle), push 2. If start at 0, stack: after DFS, push order: 1 then 0, then DFS from 2 pushes 2. Stack: bottom [0,1,2] top. Result: order may vary but consistent for SCC detection.

ladders.title: Building Block ŌĆö Graph Transposition
ladders.desc: Create the transpose of a directed graph by reversing all edges.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves generating the transpose of a directed graph, where each edge direction is reversed. The intuition is that in Kosaraju's algorithm, the transpose graph is used in the second DFS pass to identify SCCs because reversing edges changes reachability properties, helping isolate components that are strongly connected. This works because if two vertices are in the same SCC, they remain reachable in both the original and transpose graphs, but the order of processing in the transpose helps separate different SCCs. Step-by-step: 1) Represent the original graph as an adjacency list. 2) Initialize an empty adjacency list for the transpose of the same size. 3) For each vertex u in the original graph, for each neighbor v in its adjacency list, add u to the adjacency list of v in the transpose (i.e., reverse edge u->v to v->u). Time complexity is O(V+E) to iterate through all edges. Space complexity is O(V+E) for the new adjacency list. This feeds into the core logic by providing the graph needed for the second DFS pass. Common mistakes include not allocating enough space or incorrectly adding edges. Real code pattern: Use a list of lists or dictionary to build transpose.

examples.trace: Step 1: Original adjacency: 0:[1], 1:[2], 2:[]. Step 2: Initialize transpose: 0:[], 1:[], 2:[]. Step 3: For u=0, neighbor v=1: add 0 to transpose[1] ŌåÆ transpose[1]=[0]. For u=1, neighbor v=2: add 1 to transpose[2] ŌåÆ transpose[2]=[1]. For u=2, no neighbors. Result: transpose: 0:[], 1:[0], 2:[1].

Step 1: Original: 0:[1], 1:[0], 2:[2]. Step 2: Transpose init: 0:[], 1:[], 2:[]. Step 3: For u=0, v=1: add 0 to transpose[1] ŌåÆ [0]. For u=1, v=0: add 1 to transpose[0] ŌåÆ [1]. For u=2, v=2: add 2 to transpose[2] ŌåÆ [2]. Result: transpose: 0:[1], 1:[0], 2:[2].

ladders.title: Basic Operation ŌĆö Depth-First Search (DFS) on a Graph
ladders.desc: Perform DFS traversal to visit all reachable vertices from a start node.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves implementing depth-first search (DFS) to traverse a graph starting from a given vertex, visiting all vertices reachable via edges. The intuition is that DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, which is fundamental for graph algorithms like Kosaraju's. This works by using recursion or a stack to manage the exploration order, ensuring each vertex is visited exactly once. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize a visited array of size V, set all to false. 2) Define a DFS function that takes a vertex u: mark u as visited, then for each neighbor v of u, if v is not visited, recursively call DFS on v. 3) To traverse the entire graph, call DFS on each unvisited vertex. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the visited array and recursion stack. This feeds into graph transposition and core logic by providing the traversal mechanism. Common mistakes include infinite recursion in cycles (handled by visited array) or not exploring disconnected components. Real code pattern: Use adjacency list and recursive function.

examples.trace: Step 1: Visited: [false, false, false]. Step 2: DFS(0): mark visited[0]=true, neighbors: [1]. DFS(1): mark visited[1]=true, neighbors: [2]. DFS(2): mark visited[2]=true, neighbors: []. Return to DFS(1), then DFS(0). Result: all vertices visited.

Step 1: Visited: [false, false, false]. Step 2: DFS(0): mark true, neighbors: [1]. DFS(1): mark true, neighbors: [0] (already visited). Return. Vertex 2 not visited because not reachable from 0. Result: visited 0 and 1.

testCases.input: Graph: 0 vertices, edges: []; start at any

ladders.title: Concept Foundation ŌĆö Graph Representation Using Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Represent a directed graph as an adjacency list for efficient traversal.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept involves representing a directed graph using an adjacency list, where each vertex has a list of its outgoing neighbors. The intuition is that this data structure allows efficient access to neighbors, which is crucial for algorithms like DFS and Kosaraju's. This works by storing, for each vertex, a dynamic list or array of vertices that can be reached directly via an edge. Step-by-step: 1) Determine the number of vertices V. 2) Create an array or list of size V, where each element is initially an empty list. 3) For each directed edge from vertex u to vertex v, add v to the list at index u. Time complexity to build is O(E) if edges are given. Space complexity is O(V+E) to store the lists. This feeds into basic operations like DFS by providing the graph structure to traverse. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indexing or not handling duplicate edges. Real code pattern: Use a list of lists in Python, e.g., graph = [[] for _ in range(V)].

examples.trace: Step 1: V=3. Step 2: Initialize graph = [[], [], []]. Step 3: Edge 0->1: add 1 to graph[0] ŌåÆ graph[0]=[1]. Edge 1->2: add 2 to graph[1] ŌåÆ graph[1]=[2]. Result: graph = [[1], [2], []].
Step 1: V=2. Step 2: graph = [[], []]. Step 3: Edge 0->1: add 1 to graph[0] ŌåÆ [1]. Edge 1->0: add 0 to graph[1] ŌåÆ [0]. Result: graph = [[1], [0]].

approaches.name: Tarjan's Algorithm O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Use a single DFS with low-link values and a stack to identify SCCs in one pass by tracking discovery times and the lowest reachable ancestor. Construct the condensation graph by assigning each SCC a unique identifier and creating edges between SCCs from the original graph's edges. This is optimal and commonly implemented in practice.

ladders.title: Full Problem ŌĆö Build Condensation Graph Using Tarjan's Algorithm
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to find SCCs and construct the DAG of SCCs from a directed graph.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding strongly connected components (SCCs) in a directed graph and constructing a condensation graph where each SCC becomes a single node, with edges between SCCs based on original edges. Tarjan's algorithm is optimal for this task, running in O(V+E) time and O(V+E) space. The intuition is that during a single DFS, we can identify SCCs by tracking each node's discovery time and the lowest discovery time reachable from it (low-link value). When a node's low-link equals its discovery time, it's the root of an SCC, and all nodes above it on the stack (with higher low-link values) belong to the same SCC. After identifying SCCs, we assign each a unique ID and create edges between SCCs by iterating through original edges, avoiding self-loops. The algorithm uses arrays for discovery times, low-link values, stack membership, and SCC IDs, plus a stack to track visited nodes. Common mistakes include forgetting to mark nodes as on-stack, incorrectly updating low-link values during back edges, or creating duplicate edges in the condensation graph. This connects to L1 by integrating the SCC-finding subroutine with graph reconstruction.

examples.output: Condensation graph: SCCs: {0,1,2} as node A, {3,4} as node B; edges: AŌåÆB
examples.trace: Initialize arrays: disc=[-1]*5, low=[0]*5, onStack=[False]*5, sccId=[-1]*5, time=0, stack=[]. DFS from 0: disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, push 0, stack=[0]. Neighbor 1: disc[1]=1, low[1]=1, push 1, stack=[0,1]. Neighbor 2: disc[2]=2, low[2]=2, push 2, stack=[0,1,2]. Neighbor 0: visited, onStack[0]=True, low[2]=min(2,0)=0. Backtrack: low[1]=min(1,0)=0, low[0]=min(0,0)=0. At node 0, disc[0]==low[0], pop stack until 0: pop 2, sccId[2]=0; pop 1, sccId[1]=0; pop 0, sccId[0]=0; stack=[]. DFS from 3: disc[3]=3, low[3]=3, push 3, stack=[3]. Neighbor 4: disc[4]=4, low[4]=4, push 4, stack=[3,4]. Neighbor 3: visited, onStack[3]=True, low[4]=min(4,3)=3. Backtrack: low[3]=min(3,3)=3. At node 3, disc[3]==low[3], pop stack until 3: pop 4, sccId[4]=1; pop 3, sccId[3]=1; stack=[]. Build condensation: SCC IDs: 0 for nodes 0,1,2; 1 for nodes 3,4. Edges: 0ŌåÆ1 (from 1ŌåÆ3), no others (avoid self-loops). Result: 2 SCC nodes with edge from SCC0 to SCC1.

examples.output: Condensation graph: Single SCC {0,1,2,3} as node A; no edges
examples.trace: Initialize arrays for 4 nodes. DFS from 0: disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, push 0. Neighbor 1: disc[1]=1, low[1]=1, push 1. Neighbor 2: disc[2]=2, low[2]=2, push 2. Neighbor 3: disc[3]=3, low[3]=3, push 3. Neighbor 0: visited, onStack[0]=True, low[3]=min(3,0)=0. Backtrack: low[2]=min(2,0)=0, low[1]=min(1,0)=0, low[0]=min(0,0)=0. At node 0, disc[0]==low[0], pop stack until 0: pop 3, sccId[3]=0; pop 2, sccId[2]=0; pop 1, sccId[1]=0; pop 0, sccId[0]=0. No other nodes. Build condensation: All nodes in SCC ID 0, no edges between SCCs. Result: 1 SCC node.

testCases.expected: Condensation graph: SCCs: {0} as A, {1} as B; edge: AŌåÆB
Condensation graph: Single SCC {0,1,2} as A
Condensation graph: SCCs: {0} as A, {1,2} as B, {3} as C; edges: AŌåÆB, CŌåÆA
Condensation graph: SCCs: {0,1} as A, {2,3,4} as B; no edges
Condensation graph: Single SCC {0,1,2,3,4,5} as A
Condensation graph: SCCs: {0} as A, {1} as B, {2} as C; edges: AŌåÆB, BŌåÆC
Condensation graph: SCCs: {0} as A, {1,2,3} as B; edge: AŌåÆB
Condensation graph: SCCs: {0,1,2} as A, {3,4} as B; edges: AŌåÆB

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine ŌĆö Find SCCs Using Tarjan's DFS
ladders.desc: Implement Tarjan's algorithm to identify strongly connected components in a directed graph.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine performs a DFS to compute SCCs by assigning each node a discovery time and low-link value. The intuition is that nodes in the same SCC will have the same low-link value, which represents the earliest discovered node reachable from them. During DFS, when we encounter a back edge to a node on the stack, we update the low-link to propagate reachability. When a node's low-link equals its discovery time, it is the root of an SCC, and we pop nodes from the stack until reaching this root, assigning them the same SCC ID. The algorithm uses arrays: disc for discovery times (initialized to -1), low for low-link values, onStack to track nodes in the current DFS stack, and sccId for component IDs. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each node and edge is processed once; space is O(V) for arrays and stack. This feeds into L0 by providing SCC IDs needed to build the condensation graph. Common mistakes include not resetting onStack when popping or incorrectly handling cross edges. Real code pattern involves a recursive DFS function that updates low based on neighbors.

examples.output: SCC IDs: all nodes in SCC 0
examples.trace: Initialize disc=[-1,-1,-1], low=[0,0,0], onStack=[False,False,False], sccId=[-1,-1,-1], time=0, stack=[]. DFS(0): disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, onStack[0]=True, stack=[0]. Neighbor 1: DFS(1): disc[1]=1, low[1]=1, onStack[1]=True, stack=[0,1]. Neighbor 2: DFS(2): disc[2]=2, low[2]=2, onStack[2]=True, stack=[0,1,2]. Neighbor 0: visited, onStack[0]=True, low[2]=min(2,0)=0. Backtrack from 2: return. Backtrack from 1: low[1]=min(1, low[2]=0)=0. Backtrack from 0: low[0]=min(0, low[1]=0)=0. At node 0, disc[0]==low[0], pop stack: pop 2, sccId[2]=0; pop 1, sccId[1]=0; pop 0, sccId[0]=0. Result: sccId=[0,0,0].

examples.output: SCC IDs: each node in separate SCC: 0,1,2,3
examples.trace: Initialize for 4 nodes. DFS(0): disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, push 0. Neighbor 1: DFS(1): disc[1]=1, low[1]=1, push 1. Neighbor 2: DFS(2): disc[2]=2, low[2]=2, push 2. Neighbor 3: DFS(3): disc[3]=3, low[3]=3, push 3. No further neighbors. At node 3, disc[3]==low[3], pop 3, sccId[3]=3. Backtrack to 2: disc[2]==low[2], pop 2, sccId[2]=2. Backtrack to 1: disc[1]==low[1], pop 1, sccId[1]=1. Backtrack to 0: disc[0]==low[0], pop 0, sccId[0]=0. Result: sccId=[0,1,2,3].

ladders.title: Core Logic ŌĆö Compute Low-link Values in DFS
ladders.desc: Calculate the low-link value for each node during DFS to determine reachability.
ladders.explanation: The core technique is maintaining a low-link value for each node, defined as the smallest discovery time of any node reachable from that node, including itself. This is computed during DFS by initializing low[u] = disc[u] when node u is first visited, then updating it based on neighbors: if neighbor v is unvisited, recursively DFS v and set low[u] = min(low[u], low[v]); if v is visited and on the stack, set low[u] = min(low[u], disc[v]). The intuition is that low-link values propagate through back edges to identify cycles: if a node can reach an earlier node, its low-link decreases, grouping nodes into SCCs. This is done in O(V+E) time as each edge is checked once, with O(V) space for arrays. It connects to L1 by being the key step in Tarjan's algorithm for SCC detection. Common mistakes include using low[v] instead of disc[v] for back edges or not checking onStack. Real code uses a recursive function that updates low after exploring neighbors.

examples.input: Graph: 3 nodes, edges: 0ŌåÆ1, 1ŌåÆ2, 2ŌåÆ0; start DFS from 0
examples.trace: Initialize disc=[-1,-1,-1], low=[0,0,0], onStack=[False,False,False], time=0. DFS(0): disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, onStack[0]=True. Neighbor 1: DFS(1): disc[1]=1, low[1]=1, onStack[1]=True. Neighbor 2: DFS(2): disc[2]=2, low[2]=2, onStack[2]=True. Neighbor 0: visited, onStack[0]=True, update low[2]=min(2, disc[0]=0)=0. Backtrack to 1: update low[1]=min(1, low[2]=0)=0. Backtrack to 0: update low[0]=min(0, low[1]=0)=0. Final low: [0,0,0].

examples.input: Graph: 4 nodes, edges: 0ŌåÆ1, 1ŌåÆ2, 2ŌåÆ3; start DFS from 0
examples.trace: Initialize for 4 nodes. DFS(0): disc[0]=0, low[0]=0, onStack[0]=True. Neighbor 1: DFS(1): disc[1]=1, low[1]=1, onStack[1]=True. Neighbor 2: DFS(2): disc[2]=2, low[2]=2, onStack[2]=True. Neighbor 3: DFS(3): disc[3]=3, low[3]=3, onStack[3]=True. No back edges. Backtrack: low[2]=min(2, low[3]=3)=2, low[1]=min(1, low[2]=2)=1, low[0]=min(0, low[1]=1)=0. Final low: [0,1,2,3].

ladders.title: Building Block ŌĆö DFS with Discovery Times and Stack Tracking
ladders.desc: Perform DFS on a directed graph while recording discovery times and managing a stack of visited nodes.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves a DFS traversal that assigns each node a discovery time (the order it is visited) and maintains a stack to track nodes in the current DFS path. The intuition is that discovery times help order nodes, and the stack allows us to identify back edges (edges to nodes already on the stack) which indicate cycles. The algorithm: initialize an array disc with -1 (unvisited), a time counter, and an empty stack. For each unvisited node, call DFS: set disc[node] = time, increment time, push node onto stack, and mark it as on-stack. Then, for each neighbor, if unvisited, recurse; if visited and on-stack, it's a back edge. After processing neighbors, if no back edges lead to earlier nodes, the node may be popped later. Time is O(V+E), space O(V) for disc and stack. This connects to L2 by providing the framework for computing low-link values. Common mistakes include not incrementing time correctly or forgetting to mark nodes as on-stack. Real code uses recursion or an explicit stack.

examples.input: Graph: 3 nodes, edges: 0ŌåÆ1, 1ŌåÆ2; start DFS from 0
examples.output: disc: [0,1,2]; stack during DFS: [0,1,2] then popped in reverse
examples.trace: Initialize disc=[-1,-1,-1], time=0, stack=[]. DFS(0): disc[0]=0, time=1, push 0, stack=[0]. Neighbor 1: DFS(1): disc[1]=1, time=2, push 1, stack=[0,1]. Neighbor 2: DFS(2): disc[2]=2, time=3, push 2, stack=[0,1,2]. No more neighbors for 2, return. Back to 1: no more neighbors, return. Back to 0: no more neighbors, return. Final disc: [0,1,2]; stack emptied after DFS.

examples.input: Graph: 3 nodes, edges: 0ŌåÆ1, 1ŌåÆ2, 2ŌåÆ0; start DFS from 0
examples.output: disc: [0,1,2]; stack: [0,1,2] with back edge detected
examples.trace: Initialize as above. DFS(0): disc[0]=0, time=1, push 0, stack=[0]. Neighbor 1: DFS(1): disc[1]=1, time=2, push 1, stack=[0,1]. Neighbor 2: DFS(2): disc[2]=2, time=3, push 2, stack=[0,1,2]. Neighbor 0: visited and on-stack (since 0 in stack), back edge detected. Then backtrack similarly. Final disc: [0,1,2]; stack remains until SCC processing.

testCases.expected: disc: [0,1,2,3] for node 3 if present; stack includes back edge
disc: [0,1,2,3,4] if all visited; stack varies

ladders.title: Basic Operation ŌĆö Graph Representation and DFS Traversal
ladders.desc: Represent a directed graph and perform a basic depth-first search to visit all nodes.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves representing a directed graph, typically using an adjacency list (a list of lists where index i has neighbors of node i), and performing DFS to traverse all nodes. The intuition is that DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, which is efficient for graph traversal. The algorithm: initialize a visited array of size V (number of nodes) set to False. For each node, if not visited, call DFS: mark node as visited, then for each neighbor in adjacency list, if not visited, recurse. This ensures all nodes are visited in O(V+E) time, as each node and edge is processed once; space is O(V) for visited and recursion stack. This connects to L3 by adding discovery times and stack tracking. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs or using an incorrect graph representation. Real code uses a simple recursive function or iterative stack.

examples.input: Graph: 3 nodes, edges: 0ŌåÆ1, 1ŌåÆ2; adjacency list: [[1],[2],[]]; start DFS from 0
examples.trace: Initialize visited=[False,False,False]. DFS(0): mark visited[0]=True. Neighbors: [1], DFS(1): mark visited[1]=True. Neighbors: [2], DFS(2): mark visited[2]=True. Neighbors: [], return. Return to 1, then 0. All nodes visited.

examples.input: Graph: 4 nodes, edges: 0ŌåÆ1, 2ŌåÆ3; adjacency list: [[1],[],[3],[]]; start DFS from 0
examples.trace: Initialize visited=[False,False,False,False]. DFS(0): mark visited[0]=True, DFS(1): mark visited[1]=True. Node 2 unvisited: DFS(2): mark visited[2]=True, DFS(3): mark visited[3]=True. All nodes visited.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation ŌĆö Directed Graphs and Adjacency Lists
ladders.desc: Understand directed graphs and represent them using adjacency lists.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite: a directed graph consists of nodes (vertices) and edges with direction, where an edge from node u to node v means u points to v. An adjacency list is a common representation: an array or list where each element corresponds to a node and contains a list of its outgoing neighbors. For example, for a graph with 3 nodes and edges 0ŌåÆ1 and 1ŌåÆ2, the adjacency list is [[1], [2], []]. This is space-efficient for sparse graphs, using O(V+E) space. The intuition is that it allows quick access to neighbors for traversal algorithms like DFS. Time to build is O(E) if edges are given. This connects to L4 as the data structure for DFS. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indexing or not initializing empty lists for nodes with no edges. Real code in Python uses a list of lists, initialized with empty lists for each node.

examples.trace: Create a list of 3 empty lists: adj = [[], [], []]. For edge 0ŌåÆ1: append 1 to adj[0], so adj[0] = [1]. For edge 1ŌåÆ2: append 2 to adj[1], so adj[1] = [2]. adj[2] remains []. Final: [[1], [2], []].

Create adj with 4 empty lists: [[], [], [], []]. Edge 0ŌåÆ1: append 1 to adj[0] ŌåÆ [[1], [], [], []]. Edge 1ŌåÆ0: append 0 to adj[1] ŌåÆ [[1], [0], [], []]. Edge 2ŌåÆ3: append 3 to adj[2] ŌåÆ [[1], [0], [3], []]. Final: [[1], [0], [3], []].
